300.prompt: Start with this detail: They separate pedestrian movement from traffic. Which idea gives the best comparison?
wrongAnswers: Accessibility and universal design
Parking and car-centered policy
Navigation and wayfinding
300.visibleConnection: The question starts from a concrete detail in Sidewalks as urban infrastructure: They separate pedestrian movement from traffic. It is really asking which answer handles a similar pressure. Walkability is the best connection because it turns city design into daily freedom or daily friction; distance is not just measured in meters but in safety and comfort. Both ideas ask alpacas to notice that Sidewalks look ordinary because they are everywhere. That ordinariness hides how much access they quietly decide.

300.prompt: Which idea gives the clearest comparison to Accessibility and universal design?
wrongAnswers: Navigation and wayfinding
Hostile architecture and exclusion
Sidewalks as urban infrastructure
300.visibleConnection: The question starts from a concrete detail in Accessibility and universal design: Curb cuts and ramps help wheelchair users, parents with strollers, and delivery workers. It is really asking which answer handles a similar pressure. Parking and car-centered policy is the best connection because it lets stationary cars organize moving people, giving storage for vehicles more power than sidewalks, shops, or homes. Both ideas ask alpacas to notice that Accessibility is not an add-on. It exposes whether the original design imagined enough kinds of people.
